title: IMA seeks specialist-only prescriptions amid misuse risks and cheap generics of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs
summary: The Indian Medical Association is seeking government intervention to regulate GLP-1 weight-loss drugs. Concerns are rising over their misuse by various practitioners for cosmetic weight loss. The IMA advocates for restricting prescriptions to specialists to...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Mumbai: Amid a surge in misuse and mis-prescription of GLP1 weight-loss drugs, and with risks set to widen as cheaper generics enter the market, the Indian Medical Association plans to approach the government and the drug regulator to tighten controls.

IMA, the country’s largest organisation of modern medicine practitioners, is planning to seek a formal mandate restricting prescriptions of GLP-1 drugs to certified endocrinologists/diabetologists or MD general medicine practitioners to curb indiscriminate use and safeguard patient safety as access expands, Anilkumar J Nayak, national president, IMA, said in an exclusive interaction.

ET was the first to report in August last year about rampant misuse of GLP1 weight loss drugs by cosmetologists, physiotherapists, dermatologists, general MBBS clinicians, ayurveda practitioners, and other non-modern medicine practitioners.

“Many MBBS, physiotherapists and non-modern medicine practitioners are prescribing GLP1 drugs to people who neither have diabetes or any comorbidity or acute obesity but purely for cosmetic reasons to lose some weight that can be otherwise easily done with some lifestyle change like exercise and diet,” said Nayak.

“It is the duty of the government to take care of it because there is a lot of misuse and mis-prescription that needs to be curbed immediately because these medicines also have side effects. It also needs to be supplemented with proper exercise and nutrition that must be recommended by experts” he said, adding, “We will write to the government to take necessary action to stop misuse of the drug. We will discuss it in our meeting in the first week of April.”

The Drugs Controller of India has already intensified its regulatory surveillance across the GLP-1 weight loss drugs supply chain, focussing on curbing unauthorised sale and promotion.

Similar concerns over misuse and unsafe access have been previously seen globally too with the USFDA reporting hundreds of dosing errors leading to hospitalisations.

About 40 companies are planning to launch low-cost GLP 1 drug in India over the next few months as the innovator Novo Nordisk’s patent for its semaglutide molecule expired on Saturday in India.

Companies have come up with aggressive pricing as low as below Rs 1,300 for a month's treatment, or more than an 85% discount to innovator price that is expected to significantly enhance affordability and access beyond the metros.

As per industry estimates, the market for GLP1 drugs in India could expand 15-20% in the next 12-18 months from 4-5% now, as the generic versions are more affordable. India has over 100 million diabetics and 250 million obese individuals.
